figures.caption: Figure 2: Overview of GeoTopoDiff. The observed top and bottom µCT slices are converted into a topology-aware partial boundary graph. GeoTopoDiff performs mixed continuous–discrete graph diffusion over pore-body geometry and pore-throat topology. Boundary graph information is injected during denoising, and directly observed boundary variables are clamped during reverse sampling.
Table 7: Shared graph-state and diffusion settings used in GeoTopoDiff. These settings define the mixed graph state space and the categorical corruption process.

Table 2: Key sparse boundary reconstruction results for anisotropic polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and Fontainebleau sandstone. PSD error, Graph Topology Error, permeability mean relative error, and disconnected rate are reported in mean ± standard deviation over N independent held-out volumes. Lower is better for all metrics. Best results are in bold and second-best results are underlined.

Figure 1: Overview of GeoTopoDiff. Sparsely observed top and bottom µCT slices are segmented and encoded as a partial topological boundary graph. GeoTopoDiff performs topology-aware graph diffusion in a mixed state space of continuous pore geometry and discrete pore-throat topology, and decodes the denoised graph into a 3D porous structure while enforcing boundary consistency.
Table 3: Transport-oriented validation. Lower is better. PTFE measured values are from the current experiments.

Figure 3: PTFE reconstruction results shown as a representative example. The figure combines a) 3D reconstruction and orthogonal slices, 2) directional TPCF curves, and 3) transport-oriented visualisation. Compared with the strongest baseline LDM-3DG and the representative porous reconstruction baseline SliceGAN, GeoTopoDiff better preserves anisotropic channel continuity, matches the reference TPCF more closely, especially at medium and long distances, and yields transport paths that are more consistent with the reference flow field.

Figure 4: Ablation and sparsity robustness on anisotropic PTFE. (a) Qualitative comparison of the reference, the full GeoTopoDiff, and four ablated variants. Model 1 removes the boundary graph prior, Model 2 removes discrete topology diffusion, Model 3 removes geo–topology interaction, and Model 4 removes boundary cross-attention. The full model better preserves pore morphology and cross-plane continuity in the 3D rendering and orthogonal slices, while ablated variants show fragmented channels, enlarged isolated pores, or boundary-to-interior drift. (b) Robustness to increasing missing interval M . Lower is better for all metrics. The full GeoTopoDiff consistently achieves the lowest average TPCF relative error, variance, and KL divergence, and degrades more slowly as the observation gap increases.
Table 9: Topology metrics and their relevance to sparse-boundary porous reconstruction.
Table 10: Example topology diagnostics on PTFE and Fontainebleau sandstone. All metrics are computed from graphs extracted from the final binary reconstructions.
